section.title: Stewards of God's Grace
section.sectionRationale: Peter draws out the practical fruit of Christ's suffering: a decisive break with the old life of sin and a new life for God's will, lived in view of the coming judgment and the nearness of the end. That horizon presses believers not to panic but to pray, love, and serve one another as faithful stewards of God's varied grace.
section.thesis: Because Christ suffered in the body, believers arm themselves to be done with sin and live for God's will, and because the end of all things is near, they are clear-minded in prayer, fervent in love, and faithful in using their gifts as stewards of God's manifold grace, so that in everything God is glorified through Jesus Christ.
section.frameNote: Two movements: done with sin and living for God's will before the Judge, verses 1 to 6; and the nearness of the end, love and stewardship of grace, verses 7 to 11.

units.label: Done with sin, living for God's will
units.note: before the Judge of living and dead

verses.text: Therefore, since Christ suffered in His body, {{hoplizo|arm yourselves}} with the same resolve, because anyone who has suffered in his body is done with sin.
Consequently, he does not live out his remaining time on earth for human passions, but for the will of God.
For you have spent enough time in the past carrying out the same desires as the Gentiles: living in debauchery, lust, drunkenness, orgies, carousing, and detestable idolatry.
Because of this, they consider it strange of you not to plunge with them into the same flood of reckless indiscretion, and they heap abuse on you.
But they will have to give an account to Him who is ready to judge the living and the dead.
That is why the gospel was preached even to those who are now dead, so that they might be judged as men in the flesh, but live according to God in the spirit.

units.label: Love and serve as stewards of grace
units.note: the end of all things is near

verses.text: The {{telos|end of all things}} is near. Therefore be clear-minded and sober, so that you can pray.
Above all, {{agape|love one another deeply}}, because love covers over a multitude of sins.
Show hospitality to one another without complaining.
As good {{oikonomoi|stewards}} of the {{poikile|manifold grace}} of God, each of you should use whatever {{charisma|gift}} he has received to serve one another.

If anyone speaks, he should speak as one conveying the words of God. If anyone serves, he should serve with the strength God provides, so that in all things God may be glorified through Jesus Christ, to whom be the glory and the power forever and ever. Amen.

hoplizo.gloss: To arm oneself, to take up weapons for battle. Believers are to arm themselves with the same mind or resolve that Christ had in His suffering. The Christian life is pictured as warfare, and the weapon is a settled willingness to suffer rather than sin, a resolve that has broken decisively with the old life.

telos.gloss: The end, the goal or consummation. Peter says the end of all things has drawn near. This is not a cause for panic but for clear-headed prayer and love. The nearness of the end gives the present its weight and urgency, focusing the church on what truly matters as history moves toward its appointed goal.

agape.gloss: Self-giving love that seeks the good of others. Peter says, above all, love one another earnestly, for love covers a multitude of sins. In a community under pressure, fervent love is the first priority, bearing with one another's faults and knitting the church together as the end draws near.

oikonomoi.gloss: A steward, a household manager entrusted with another's resources. Believers are stewards of God's grace, responsible to use what He has given for the good of others. A steward owns nothing and must give account; so gifts are held in trust from God and deployed for the household of faith, not for self.

poikile.gloss: Manifold, many-colored, varied grace. God's grace comes in diverse forms, distributed in different gifts to different believers. The word pictures grace like a richly varied tapestry; no one holds it all, so the church needs every member's gift to display the full spectrum of God's giving.

charisma.gloss: A gift of grace, a Spirit-given ability for service. Each believer has received some charisma to use in serving others. The gifts are not for personal display but for the common good, so that whether one speaks or serves, God is glorified through Jesus Christ, the giver and goal of every gift.

basic.context: Because Christ suffered, Peter says, arm yourselves with the same resolve: be done with sin and live the rest of your life for God's will, not for the old cravings. Your former friends think it strange that you no longer join their wild living, and they insult you, but they will answer to God, the Judge of the living and the dead. Then Peter lifts the horizon: the end of all things is near. So keep clear heads and pray. Above all, love one another deeply, be hospitable, and use whatever gift God has given you to serve others, as faithful stewards of His varied grace, so that in everything God gets the glory through Jesus Christ.
basic.hermeneutics: Peter uses the coming judgment and the nearness of the end not to stir fear or speculation but to fuel holy, loving, useful living now. Two lines need care. Anyone who has suffered in the body is done with sin, verse 1, means the settled resolve to suffer rather than sin marks a decisive break with the old life. And the gospel preached to those now dead, verse 6, most likely means believers who heard the gospel while alive and have since died, so that though judged in the body by death, they live before God.
basic.originalLanguages: Since Christ suffered, believers hoplizo, arm themselves, with the same resolve, done with sin. The telos, the end of all things, is near, so they pray and above all show agape, love, which covers many sins. As oikonomoi, stewards, of the poikile charis, the manifold grace of God, each uses his charisma, his gift, to serve others.
basic.typology: The steward of the household reflects the whole biblical picture of God's people as managers of what He owns, from Adam tending the garden to servants entrusted with talents. And the coming Judge of the living and the dead fulfils the Old Testament expectation of the day of the Lord, now centered on Christ.
basic.christ: Christ's suffering is the pattern and power for the believer's break with sin. He is the one through whom God is glorified in all our serving, and to whom belong glory and power forever. As the Judge of the living and the dead, He is the one before whom both mockers and martyrs will finally stand.
basic.god: God is the Judge of the living and the dead, before whom all give account. He is the giver of manifold grace and varied gifts, and the one who supplies the strength for service. He is the God whose will now directs the believer's life and whose glory is the goal of everything. He is generous, sovereign, and worthy of all glory and power forever.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 6 speaks of dying to sin and living to God. Romans 12 and 1 Corinthians 12 describe varied gifts for serving the body. Proverbs 10 says love covers all offenses. And James 5 says the coming of the Lord is near, urging patient, prayerful living.
basic.oneStory: The whole Bible moves toward the end, when the Judge of all sets things right. Christ has suffered and risen, sin's grip is broken, and the church now lives in the last stretch of the story. In this in-between time God's people love, pray, and serve as stewards of His grace, so that when the end comes, He is glorified through Jesus Christ, to whom belong glory and power forever.

context.literary: The therefore of verse 1 flows from the suffering and exaltation of Christ in 3:18 to 22. Verses 1 to 6 contrast the old life of the Gentiles with the new life for God's will, framed by the coming judgment. Verse 7 pivots on the nearness of the end into a series of communal commands, climaxing in a doxology at verse 11 that closes the first main part of the letter's exhortation.
context.historical: The readers had come out of a pagan lifestyle their neighbors still lived, and their withdrawal from the old debauchery drew ridicule and abuse. Peter names the vices frankly. Hospitality was vital in a world with few inns and traveling believers needing shelter, and the varied gifts show a functioning house-church life where every member had a part to play.
context.canonical: The passage joins the New Testament theme of the nearness of the end shaping present conduct, and its teaching on stewardship of gifts stands alongside Romans 12 and 1 Corinthians 12. The closing doxology, glory and power to God through Christ forever, is a model of how eschatological expectation issues in worship rather than mere speculation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To press the suffering and reign of Christ into a decisive break with sin and a life lived for God's will, and to use the nearness of the end to fuel clear-minded prayer, fervent love, and faithful stewardship of gifts, so that in all things God is glorified through Jesus Christ.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The section mixes both. It describes the believer's break with the old life and the coming judgment, and on that basis prescribes: arm yourselves, live for God's will, pray, love, show hospitality, serve. The doxology turns prescription into praise, showing that the goal of all obedience is God's glory.

debates.question: What does anyone who has suffered in the body is done with sin mean, verse 1?

views.label: A settled resolve to suffer rather than sin
views.case: To arm oneself with Christ's mind is to be so committed to God's will that one would rather suffer than sin. Whoever has made that break has decisively turned from the old life; suffering for righteousness marks the end of sin's reign.

views.label: Union with Christ's death to sin
views.case: Others hear an echo of dying with Christ: the believer who has suffered with Christ has, in principle, died to sin's dominion, as in Romans 6. Either way the point is a real break with sin, not sinless perfection.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm a decisive turning from sin grounded in Christ, not a claim that believers never sin again. The armed, suffering resolve of the Christian is incompatible with a settled life of sin.

debates.question: Who are those now dead to whom the gospel was preached, verse 6?

views.label: Believers who heard the gospel and have since died
views.case: The most common reading: they heard the good news while alive, and though they have died, judged as all men are by bodily death, they live before God in the spirit. The verse comforts a church whose members were dying under pressure.

views.label: A proclamation connected to Christ's descent
views.case: Some link it to 3:19, seeing a proclamation to the departed. Yet even here orthodox readers deny a second chance after death; the emphasis remains that the gospel secures life with God beyond bodily death.

debates.bearing: Both guard against the idea of a second chance after death. The verse assures believers that death does not defeat those who received the gospel; judged in the body, they nonetheless live to God in the spirit.

study.originalLanguages: Because Christ suffered in the body, believers hoplizo, arm themselves, with the same resolve, so committed to God's will that they are done with sin and no longer live for the old passions. Their former companions revile them, but all must answer to the Judge of living and dead. The telos, the end of all things, has drawn near, so believers stay clear-minded for prayer and, above all, show earnest agape, love that covers a multitude of sins, with ungrudging hospitality. As oikonomoi, stewards, of the poikile charis, the varied, many-colored grace of God, each deploys his charisma, his grace-gift, to serve others, whether in speaking God's words or serving in His strength, so that in everything God is glorified through Jesus Christ.

typology.type: Stewards of the household of grace
typology.fulfillment: God's people as managers of what He owns, from Adam in the garden to the servants entrusted with talents, called to faithful use of every gift until the Master returns.

typology.type: The Judge of the living and the dead
typology.fulfillment: The Old Testament day of the Lord, when God sets all things right, now centered on Christ before whom mockers and martyrs alike will stand.

study.christ: Christ's suffering in the body is both the pattern and the power for the believer's decisive break with sin; to arm oneself with the same resolve is to share His mind. He is the one through whom God is glorified in all Christian service, and to whom belong the glory and the power forever, as the closing doxology declares. And He is the Judge of the living and the dead, ready to call every mocker and every faithful sufferer to account. The suffering Savior is also the glorified Lord and coming Judge.
study.god: God is revealed as the righteous Judge of the living and the dead, before whom all give account, so that the abuse believers now suffer will be answered at His tribunal. He is also the generous giver of manifold grace, distributing varied gifts and supplying the very strength for service. His will now directs the believer's remaining time on earth, and His glory is the goal of everything. He is just, lavishly gracious, and worthy of glory and power forever.

crossReferences.note: Dying to sin and living to God with Christ, the decisive break behind arm yourselves and being done with sin.
A variety of gifts from the one Spirit for the common good, paralleling the manifold grace and varied gifts of verse 10.
Love covers all offenses, the proverb behind love covers over a multitude of sins in verse 8.
The coming of the Lord is near, so be patient and prayerful, matching Peter's use of the nearness of the end.

study.oneStory: The whole Bible presses toward the end, when the Judge of all will set things right and God's people will live fully with Him. Christ has suffered, risen, and reigns, so sin's grip is broken and the church lives in the final stretch of the story. In this in-between time believers arm themselves against sin, pray with clear minds, love one another deeply, and serve as stewards of God's varied grace. The aim of it all is that when the end arrives, God is found glorified through Jesus Christ, to whom belong glory and power forever.

quiz.q: What does it mean to arm yourselves with the same resolve as Christ?

options.text: To be so committed to God's will that you would rather suffer than sin.
options.note: Verse 1: the armed resolve is a decisive break with the old life of sin.

options.text: To seek out suffering for its own sake.
options.note: The point is willingness to suffer rather than sin, not seeking suffering itself.

options.text: To defend yourself aggressively against all opponents.
options.note: The weapon is a mind set on God's will, not aggression toward others.

options.text: To share Christ's death to sin and live for God.
options.note: True to the sense, though the answer should name the resolve to suffer rather than sin.

quiz.q: How do the readers' former companions react to their new life?

options.text: They think it strange and heap abuse on them for not joining the old excess.
options.note: Verse 4: withdrawal from debauchery draws ridicule and slander.

options.text: They admire and quickly imitate the change.
options.note: Peter says the opposite: they are surprised and abusive.

options.text: They ignore the believers entirely.
options.note: They do not ignore them; they revile them.

options.text: They are surprised and respond with insults.
options.note: True, though the answer should include that they will answer to the Judge.

quiz.q: What effect does the nearness of the end have, according to Peter?

options.text: It calls for clear-minded, sober prayer and fervent love, not panic.
options.note: Verses 7 to 8: the end fuels prayer and love, not speculation or fear.

options.text: It means believers should stop working and simply wait.
options.note: Peter calls for active love, hospitality, and service, not passivity.

options.text: It should provoke fearful calculation of dates.
options.note: The response is prayer and love, not date-setting.

options.text: It gives urgency to loving and serving one another.
options.note: True and close, though the answer should include clear-minded prayer as well.

quiz.q: How are believers to use the gifts they have received?

options.text: As stewards of God's manifold grace, using each gift to serve one another.
options.note: Verse 10: gifts are held in trust and deployed for others' good.

options.text: For personal display and recognition.
options.note: The gifts serve others and God's glory, not self-display.

options.text: Only if they feel especially talented.
options.note: Each one has received a gift to use; it is not for a gifted few only.

options.text: To build up the church according to what God has given.
options.note: True, though the answer should name stewardship of God's manifold grace.

quiz.q: What is the stated goal of all Christian speaking and serving?

options.text: That in all things God may be glorified through Jesus Christ.
options.note: Verse 11: God's glory through Christ is the aim, sealed with a doxology.

options.text: That the church may grow impressively large.
options.note: The goal is God's glory, not size or reputation.

options.text: That believers may be praised for their service.
options.note: The glory goes to God through Christ, not to the servant.

options.text: That the community may function smoothly.
options.note: Good order helps, but the stated goal is God's glory through Jesus Christ.

questions: Peter says you have spent enough time in the old way of living. What part of your former life is God calling you to leave firmly behind?
When others find your faith strange and mock your choices, how do you usually respond, and how might remembering the coming Judge steady you?
The end of all things is near. If you truly believed today might be near the end, what would you pray for, and whom would you love better?
Love covers a multitude of sins. Whose faults is God asking you to bear with in love rather than expose or resent?
You are a steward of some gift of God's grace. What has He given you, and how could you use it this week to serve someone else and glorify Him?

translationNotes.note: Done with sin renders a phrase meaning has ceased from sin; in context it describes the decisive break with the old life that marks one armed with Christ's resolve, not sinless perfection.
Those who are now dead most likely means believers who heard the gospel and have since died; judged as all are by bodily death, they nonetheless live to God in the spirit.
Manifold grace translates poikile charis, varied or many-colored grace, distributed as diverse gifts so that the whole church displays the fullness of God's giving.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Peter 4:1-11. No dashes appear in this movement's text.
